focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The first plank walk above Sump Ward bowed under Silas's boots and gave him the ward at once: warm mud, lamp slime, old soap, and the sweet-sour breath of digesters working too hard in weather that had not yet learned mercy. The boards under him sweated through their grain. Below, the drainage cut moved like thick tea, carrying peelings, reed scraps, and a pale skim of algae that caught every color the ward gave it and made none of them cleaner.

Need had sent him looking for a useful theft. He would not have called it theft, even to himself. He would have called it a principle, a pattern, one true hinge hidden under all the neighborhood babble, something he could carry back uphill and sell to men who liked their mysteries trimmed flat and lettered in gilt. He wanted one good phrase, perhaps two. He wanted enough to make the runaway moss stop embarrassing everybody by acting like a crowd.

Aunt Vero waited at the landing with a tobacco tin open in one hand, fennel seeds shining against the metal. She crunched one between her back teeth, eyed him with the mild contempt she seemed to reserve for men who still believed dry shoes were a form of virtue, and tipped the tin toward him without generosity. Her thick white braid was looped over one shoulder. One eye drifted a little off him and back again; even her face refused to spend both its sights on Silas Vale.

"He'll kiss muck inside ten yards," she said to Inez. "Let him gulp a little ward water first. Men come in dry and puffed up; one swallow of drain slop settles them lower than prayer."

Inez had her dark hair wrapped in a work scarf the color of old olives, though the damp had already darkened it nearer black. She stood easy on the planks, weight where the nails still held, one hand on a tray strap cutt...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ck into the pink, cautious and resentful, like water reclaiming dye from cloth.

Silas looked at it until the colors stopped fighting long enough for him to see the room by it: the damp patched wall, the satchel strap, the child's bandaged fingers held away from her body with offended care. Then he looked at Inez, at the old burn scar on her wrist, at the steadiness with which she stood in a house not hers and was nonetheless obeyed before anyone thought to ask why.

He would have liked to call her furious and be done with it. That would have been easy, only a mood he could have weathered. It would have asked nothing of him but endurance. What stood in front of him was worse: the kind of competence that could indict a man without raising its voice and make his whole trade look like a disease of decorative rich fools.

He set the warped tray down by the door. His cuff had gone stiff where the pink dried, a crescent badge of the mess he'd carried into other people's rooms. His palm carried the hot memory of the kettle through the cloth. Somewhere above them, on the steps between Patent Row and the river, people were still trying his word in their mouths because it amused them.

When he spoke again, there was no performance left in it.

"All right," he said. "I hear you."

Inez's expression did not change. It did not need to. She had not dragged him from the steps to win an argument. She had dragged him to put him in the same room as consequence and make him breathe it.

"Good," she said. "Now try learning faster than the city talks."

He went back into the wet air with the stink of goosefat and hot tin on him and Inez's last line following him harder than the tray handle had.

Lantern Steps emptied him toward Ferrymouth, and the word had already gotten there ahead of him.
